attack_summary: An active ICMP flood (Network DoS, T1498.001) was launched from a compromised Ubuntu host (UbuntuComputer, IP 192.168.1.3, process 'splunkd') using hping3 against a WAN/LAN target (7.0.14.254). The agent performed discovery (pinged 192.168.1.2) and maintained C2 contact to 203.0.113.1; the attacker remained active in the digital-twin.

mitigations.name: Insert pre-configured Snort IPS inline between FortiGate and LAN
mitigations.brief_description: Removed the direct FortiGate <-> cisco-switch link, added a preconfigured Snort device (snort-ids-1), and linked FortiGate -> snort-ids-1 -> cisco-switch so the inline IPS inspects LAN traffic. The Snort IPS immediately detected and blocked high-rate ICMP flood patterns while leaving the compromised host in place; legitimate LAN and Internet checks continued to succeed.

LAN Connectivity Check (Ping).description: Checks inter-network connectivity by pinging a target host on the LAN. The target IP is defined by the fact 'lan.target.ip'.

Internet Connectivity Check (HTTP).description: Deadman ability that verifies internet connectivity by making an HTTP HEAD request to a well-known public endpoint. Uses curl instead of ping (ICMP) to test TCP-based network reachability. Returns the HTTP status code.

key_observations: Attack vector: raw ICMP flood generated on-host (hping3) after successful network discovery; target IP facts collected: lan target 192.168.1.2 and icmp target 7.0.14.254.
Mitigation was minimally disruptive and aligned with live-IR constraints: the compromised host remained connected and the inline IPS stopped the flood without host quarantine.

Tool-level actions were executed with explicit topology changes (deleted FortiGate<->switch link, added snort-ids-1, created FortiGate->Snort and Snort->switch links) and the judge validated the attack success rate dropping from 1.0 to 0.0.

No Snort signature logs or dropped-packet counters were supplied; validation relies on topology change, tool responses, and judge confirmation. Recommended follow-up: inspect Snort and FortiGate logs, verify no alternate/bypass paths (e.g., host using 203.0.113.2), and confirm which rules triggered.

This approach is transferable: inserting an inline IPS to stop volumetric floods is an effective live-IR technique when host removal is prohibited; complementary controls (firewall egress filtering, host rate-limiting, DoS sensors) remain recommended for longer-term resilience.
overall_conclusion: The single, constrained live-IR action — inserting a pre-configured Snort IPS inline between the firewall and LAN — successfully mitigated the active ICMP flood while preserving the compromised host for forensic analysis and maintaining legitimate connectivity. Follow-up actions should verify IDS/firewall logs, check for bypass paths, and implement layered controls (FortiGate DoS tuning, egress filtering, host rate-limits and monitoring) to reduce recurrence risk.
